posts.title: Before September, one date matters: 27 July.
know: The court ordered the Christian Brothers to hand over comprehensive financial disclosure by 27 July 2026 — an order that survivor-side solicitors pushed for at the 2 July hearing.
That disclosure lets survivors' lawyers and other interested parties test the real financial position of the entities before anyone is asked to vote on the scheme.
The moratorium (case: Trustees of the Christian Brothers [2026] NSWSC 781) holds until the scheme is either approved or the court orders otherwise. Claims stay frozen in the meantime.

Plaintiff lawyers are pushing to substitute a new defendant into existing cases, so claims could continue against another party. Justice Nixon said he wouldn't decide that without first hearing from EREA.
The Commonwealth confirmed the National Redress Scheme stays open, with a closure date of 30 June 2027 for applications naming the order.
Nothing has been decided on the $1 EREA property transfers. The disclosure due 27 July is where that question gets its first real test.
body: It's quiet right now, and quiet is easy to mistake for nothing happening. It isn't. Behind the freeze, the fight is about information — whether the order's books show what it says they show, and whether the assets moved out for a dollar a title can be pulled back into view. The 27 July disclosure is the hinge. Everything argued in September rests on what those documents reveal.

If you have a claim, or think you might, the advice hasn't changed: talk to one of the free legal services on this page now, while the landscape is still being drawn. The people asking hard questions in court are doing it on behalf of survivors who may never see the filings. This page will keep watching the dates so they don't slip past unmarked.

posts.title: The court froze the claims. September is the next door.
know: On 2 July, Justice Scott Nixon of the NSW Supreme Court granted the moratorium — a stay pausing all civil abuse claims against the Province Entities, including settled matters still awaiting payment.
The freeze holds until a two-day hearing beginning 21 September 2026, which will settle how the proposed scheme actually works before creditors vote on it.
The judge said he paused the claims to preserve survivors' chance to consider the scheme, warning that opportunity might otherwise be lost.

Around 32 abuse matters were listed for trial within three months. Those are now suspended. More than 240 claimants are identified in the filings, and 540 applications naming the congregation sit before the National Redress Scheme.
The court heard the order's cash reserves stand at about $23M, drawing down roughly $1.7M a week in payouts since October.
The National Redress Scheme is unaffected — the Commonwealth's barrister confirmed survivors can keep applying.
Claims against EREA and other Catholic institutions are still not blocked by any of this.
body: This is the development the last entry was watching for, and it landed hard. The claims are frozen — not ended, frozen — and a survivor who was days from trial is now waiting until spring. One complainant told the Guardian they felt stabbed in the back with a long, bladed knife. Another had simply hoped to resolve their claim quickly and move on. Both are now holding.

Hold the two truths together. The court's reasoning — that a pause protects the chance to weigh the scheme — is not nothing. And the lived experience of being made to wait again, by the same institution, after decades, is also not nothing. You are allowed to feel the second even if you understand the first.

The date that matters now is 21 September. That hearing shapes the terms you'll eventually be asked to vote on. If you have a live claim, this is the moment to get advice from one of the free services on this page — not to wait and see.

posts.title: Sold for a dollar. Now there's nothing left to pay with.
know: Guardian records show land, school buildings and homes — some in Sydney worth millions — were transferred from the Christian Brothers to EREA for just $1 each, most of them in 2018.
The Australian Financial Review valued the assets moved to EREA at about $891M in late 2024, and possibly up to $2B now.
In court, the Commonwealth's barrister said the government has concerns about whether those transfers were appropriate, and that the records raise more questions than answers.
EREA says it is a separate legal entity, not responsible for the Christian Brothers' liabilities, and will not sell property to help — citing governance and fiduciary duties.
The Trustees say their scheme would itself be able to scrutinise the transfers, and that their scheme advisors were not involved in the original EREA handover.
body: Here is the arithmetic a survivor is asked to accept. The order says it owes around $774M to abuse claimants. It holds $23M in cash and $216M in property. And over the decade since the Royal Commission began, it moved assets worth hundreds of millions — by one estimate, billions — to a related entity, for a dollar a title.

EREA calls it the completion of a governance transition begun years earlier. The Commonwealth calls it a concern worth examining in court. A lawyer for one survivor called it salt in the wound. This page's job is not to settle that in advance of the court — it is to make sure the question is not quietly dropped. Follow the property.

posts.title: The week the order said it could not pay
know: The Trustees announced insolvency on 22 June and proposed a Creditors' Scheme to distribute ~$216M in remaining assets — roughly 36 properties.
This made the Christian Brothers the first Catholic order in Australia to propose winding itself out of existence over abuse liabilities.
A stay pauses claims; it does not erase them. If the scheme is approved, live claims move into its adjudication process, run by a panel of retired judges.
Survivors keep a choice: pursue the scheme, or the National Redress Scheme.
Claims against EREA and other Catholic bodies are not blocked by this process.
If creditors or the Court reject the scheme, the entities go into liquidation — and the return gets smaller.
The scheme also sets aside provision for the modest future living needs of the 176 remaining brothers, average age about 80.
body: Forty-five years and more than $480 million paid, and the message this week is that the well is nearly dry. Read it twice. The body built to make redress is now asking a court to freeze the very claims it exists to answer. That can be lawful and still feel like the floor giving way.

If you are a survivor: you have not missed your window, you have not lost your choice, and you do not have to work this out alone — the legal services listed on this page are free. Watch the moratorium. That is the next door, and what the Court does with it shapes everything after.
